subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Assalamu alaikum, young scientist! Look around you right now. The wooden stool you might be sitting on is a solid. The water in the cup beside you is a liquid. The air you are breathing in and out is a gas. Without even thinking about it, you are surrounded by all three states of matter at every single moment of your day. In this unit you will learn to see the world with new eyes, understanding why ice is hard, why water flows, and why steam rises from a pot of bariis cooking over the fire.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 5 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Name and describe the three states of matter: solid, liquid, and gas, and give examples of each from your own home.
Explain the particle model - that all matter is made of tiny particles, and describe how those particles are arranged and how they move in each state.
Describe state changes - melting, freezing, evaporation, condensing, and boiling - and explain how heating and cooling cause them.
Explain evaporation and identify the four main factors that make a liquid evaporate faster: temperature, surface area, air movement, and humidity.
Describe the water cycle and explain how evaporation and condensation move water between the ocean, the sky, and the land.
Plan a fair test to investigate what affects evaporation, and record your results carefully.
Use scientific words correctly when you write and talk about matter.
Name two other factors mentioned in the passage that help the fish dry faster.

concepts.title: The Three States of Matter
concepts.explanation: Let us begin with what you can see and touch. Take a look at three things you know well: an ice cube, a cup of water, and the air in the room. These are the three states of the very same substance - water - and yet they behave completely differently. Understanding why is the key that unlocks this whole unit. A solid keeps its own shape. If you place a stone in a bowl, it stays stone-shaped. If you move it to a bucket, it is still the same shape and the same size. A solid has a definite shape and a definite volume,…
concepts.example: A liquid is different. It still takes up a fixed amount of space - it has a definite volume - but it does not have a shape of its own. Instead, a liquid takes the shape of whatever container you pour it into. Pour water…

concepts.title: The Particle Model - The Secret Inside Matter
concepts.explanation: Now we come to one of the biggest ideas in all of science. Why does a solid keep its shape while a gas floats away? To answer that, scientists imagine that all matter is built from unbelievably tiny pieces called particles. These particles are far too small to see, even with a normal magnifying glass. A single drop of water contains billions upon billions of them. The way these particles are arranged, and how they move, explains everything about the three states. In a solid, the particles are packed very closely…
concepts.example: In a liquid, the particles are still close together, but they are no longer locked in a fixed pattern. They can slide and roll past one another, a bit like a bag of small dried beans that shift around when you tip the…

concepts.title: Changing State - The Power of Heating and Cooling
concepts.explanation: How does one state turn into another? The answer is energy, and usually that energy comes as heat. When you heat matter, you give its particles more energy, so they move faster and push further apart. When you cool matter, you take energy away, so the particles slow down and move closer together. By adding or removing heat, we can make matter change from solid to liquid to gas, and back again. When you heat a solid, its particles vibrate faster and faster. At a certain temperature, called the melting point, they…
concepts.example: Freezing is the opposite of melting. When you cool a liquid, its particles slow down. At the freezing point, they slow so much that the forces of attraction lock them back into fixed positions, and the liquid becomes a…

concepts.title: Evaporation and the Factors That Affect It
concepts.explanation: Evaporation deserves special attention because it happens all around you, all the time, and understanding it is genuinely useful. Here is the key idea: evaporation can happen at any temperature, not just at boiling point. A puddle in Garissa dries up on a warm morning long before the water is anywhere near boiling. This is because, even in cool water, a few of the fastest-moving particles at the surface always have enough energy to escape into the air. Slowly, one by one, they leave, and the puddle shrinks until…
concepts.example: The hotter the liquid or the air around it, the faster it evaporates. Heat gives the particles more energy, so more of them can escape from the surface. This is why Halima hangs the clothes out in the strong afternoon…

concepts.title: The Water Cycle - Water on a Never-Ending Journey
concepts.explanation: Now let us put evaporation and condensation together to understand one of the grandest systems on Earth: the water cycle. This is the endless journey that water takes as it moves between the ocean, the sky, and the land. It is powered by energy from the sun, and it brings the rains that fill our rivers and grow our crops. Let us follow a single drop of water on its journey. Evaporation. The sun shines on the Indian Ocean and warms the surface. Water evaporates, rising into the sky as invisible water vapour. Water…
concepts.example: Condensation and clouds. High in the sky the air is much cooler. As the water vapour rises and cools, it condenses into countless tiny water droplets. Billions of these droplets together form the clouds you see drifting…

concepts.title: Dissolving - When a Solid Seems to Disappear
concepts.explanation: There is one more everyday change to understand: dissolving. When you stir a spoonful of sugar into a cup of hot shaah, the sugar seems to vanish. Where does it go? The sugar has dissolved. It has not disappeared and it has not melted - the tiny sugar particles have spread out and slipped into the spaces between the water particles, mixing so completely that you can no longer see them. You know the sugar is still there because the tea tastes sweet. A mixture like this, where a solid is completely dissolved in a…
concepts.example: Some things dissolve and some do not. Sugar and salt dissolve well in water. Sand and small stones do not dissolve - stir them as much as you like and they still settle at the bottom. Warm water usually dissolves things…

explorations.title: Sorting the States of Matter
explorations.context: To observe and sort household materials into solids, liquids, and gases by testing their properties. Make a Prediction Before you start, write down: How will you tell a solid from a liquid? What test could show that air (a gas) is really there?
explorations.prompt: Which materials kept their own shape no matter which cup they were in?
explorations.answer: Which materials kept their own shape no matter which cup they were in? Which materials took the shape of the container but kept the same amount? How did you prove that the gas in the balloon was really there? Was any material hard to sort? Why?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To observe and sort household materials into solids, liquids, and gases by testing their properties. Make a Prediction Before you start, write down: How will you tell a solid from a liquid? What test could show that air (a gas) is really there?

explorations.title: Watching Ice Melt (A State Change)
explorations.context: To observe melting - a solid changing to a liquid - and see how a warmer place speeds it up. Make a Prediction Which ice cube will melt faster: one left in a warm sunny spot, or one left in a cool shady spot? Write your guess and why.
explorations.prompt: Which cube melted faster? Was your prediction correct?
explorations.answer: Which cube melted faster? Was your prediction correct? What is melting? Which state changed into which? Using particles, explain why heat made the ice melt faster. The puddle left behind is the same water that was frozen. What does this tell you about state changes?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To observe melting - a solid changing to a liquid - and see how a warmer place speeds it up. Make a Prediction Which ice cube will melt faster: one left in a warm sunny spot, or one left in a cool shady spot? Write your guess and why.

explorations.title: The Great Evaporation Race
explorations.context: To carry out a fair test showing how surface area affects how fast water evaporates. Make a Prediction Two equal amounts of water: one in a wide shallow dish, one in a narrow tall cup. Which will dry up first? Write your prediction and your reason.
explorations.prompt: Which container lost its water first? Was your prediction correct?
explorations.answer: Which container lost its water first? Was your prediction correct? Why is it important that both containers were in the same place with the same amount of water? Which was the one thing you changed (the variable being tested)? Explain your result using the idea of surface area and escaping…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To carry out a fair test showing how surface area affects how fast water evaporates. Make a Prediction Two equal amounts of water: one in a wide shallow dish, one in a narrow tall cup. Which will dry up first? Write your prediction and your reason.

explorations.title: Catching Condensation
explorations.context: To make condensation happen and understand that water vapour in the air can turn back into liquid. Make a Prediction What will appear on the outside of a cold glass left in the room? Where do you think that water comes from?
explorations.prompt: What appeared on the outside of the cup?
explorations.answer: What appeared on the outside of the cup? The cup did not leak. So where did this water really come from? What is this process called, and is it the opposite of evaporation? Why did the water form on a COLD cup and not on a warm one?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To make condensation happen and understand that water vapour in the air can turn back into liquid. Make a Prediction What will appear on the outside of a cold glass left in the room? Where do you think that water comes from?

explorations.title: A Mini Water Cycle in a Bowl
explorations.context: To build a small model of the water cycle and watch evaporation, condensation, and 'rain' happen. Make a Prediction If warm water sits in a covered bowl in the sun, what will happen to the water? Where will droplets appear?
explorations.prompt: Where did droplets form first, and why there?
explorations.answer: Where did droplets form first, and why there? How did water get from the warm 'ocean' up to the cling film? Name the process. How did water get from the cling film down into the empty cup? Name the two processes. How is this bowl like the real water cycle that brings the Gu rains?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To build a small model of the water cycle and watch evaporation, condensation, and 'rain' happen. Make a Prediction If warm water sits in a covered bowl in the sun, what will happen to the water? Where will droplets appear?

methods.title: Sorting the States of Matter
methods.example: To observe and sort household materials into solids, liquids, and gases by testing their properties. Make a Prediction Before you start, write down: How will you tell a solid from a liquid? What test could show that air (a gas) is really there?
steps: Take each solid and place it in different cups. Does it change shape? Does it keep its size? Record your answer.
Pour a little water into a tall cup, then into a wide bowl. Does the shape change? Does the amount change? Record it.
Do the same test with the cooking oil and the milk.
Blow air into the balloon or trap air in the bag. Squeeze it gently. Can you feel the gas pushing back? Does it fill the whole balloon?
Fill in your results table below.

methods.title: Watching Ice Melt (A State Change)
methods.example: To observe melting - a solid changing to a liquid - and see how a warmer place speeds it up. Make a Prediction Which ice cube will melt faster: one left in a warm sunny spot, or one left in a cool shady spot? Write your guess and why.
steps: Place one ice cube on each plate.
Put one plate in a warm, sunny place and the other in a cool, shady place.
Start your timer. Every 5 minutes, look at both cubes and write down how much has melted.
Record the time when each cube has completely melted.

methods.title: The Great Evaporation Race
methods.example: To carry out a fair test showing how surface area affects how fast water evaporates. Make a Prediction Two equal amounts of water: one in a wide shallow dish, one in a narrow tall cup. Which will dry up first? Write your prediction and your reason.
steps: Pour exactly the same amount of water into the wide dish and the tall cup.
Mark the starting water level on each container.
Place both in the same warm, sunny place, side by side, so temperature and wind are the same for both. The ONLY thing different is the surface area.
Check twice a day and mark the new water level with the time.
Continue until one container is empty. Record which dried up first.

methods.title: Catching Condensation
methods.example: To make condensation happen and understand that water vapour in the air can turn back into liquid. Make a Prediction What will appear on the outside of a cold glass left in the room? Where do you think that water comes from?
steps: Wipe the outside of the cup completely dry so you know it starts dry.
Fill the cup with cold water and add ice if you have it.
Place it on a table and wait 5 to 10 minutes without touching it.
Look closely at the outside of the cup. Gently touch it. What do you feel and see? Record it.

methods.title: A Mini Water Cycle in a Bowl
methods.example: To build a small model of the water cycle and watch evaporation, condensation, and 'rain' happen. Make a Prediction If warm water sits in a covered bowl in the sun, what will happen to the water? Where will droplets appear?
steps: Pour a little warm water into the bottom of the large bowl (this is your 'ocean'). Do not fill it.
Stand the empty small cup in the middle of the bowl, keeping it dry inside (this is your 'land').
Cover the whole bowl tightly with cling film so no air escapes.
Place the small stone on the cling film right above the empty cup, so the film dips down slightly toward the cup.
Put the bowl in a warm, sunny spot and watch for 30 to 60 minutes.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 1
workedExamples.prompt: Which state of matter keeps both a definite shape and a definite volume? (a) solid (b) liquid (c) gas (d) none of them
workedExamples.solution: 1. (a) solid. Only solids keep both a fixed shape and a fixed volume because their particles are locked in place.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: In which state are the particles far apart and moving quickly in all directions? (a) solid (b) liquid (c) gas (d) ice
workedExamples.solution: 2. (c) gas. Gas particles are spread far apart and move quickly in every direction.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: A liquid poured from a cup into a bowl will: (a) change its amount (b) keep its shape (c) take the shape of the bowl (d) turn into a gas
workedExamples.solution: 3. (c) take the shape of the bowl. A liquid keeps its volume but takes the container's shape; the amount does not change.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: Melting is the change from: (a) liquid to solid (b) solid to liquid (c) liquid to gas (d) gas to liquid
workedExamples.solution: 4. (b) solid to liquid. Melting is a solid becoming a liquid when heated.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: Which change needs heat to be REMOVED? (a) melting (b) evaporation (c) boiling (d) freezing
workedExamples.solution: 5. (d) freezing. Freezing needs heat removed so particles slow and lock into a solid. The others all need heat added.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Evaporation happens: (a) only at boiling point (b) at any temperature, at the surface (c) only in a freezer (d) only at night
workedExamples.solution: 6. (b) at any temperature, at the surface. Only surface particles with enough energy escape; boiling is not required.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: Which factor does NOT speed up evaporation? (a) more heat (b) more wind (c) a smaller surface area (d) drier air
workedExamples.solution: 7. (c) a smaller surface area. A smaller surface slows evaporation; the other three all speed it up.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: Water droplets forming on a cold glass is an example of: (a) melting (b) condensation (c) freezing (d) dissolving
workedExamples.solution: 8. (b) condensation. Water vapour in the air cools on the glass and turns to liquid.

practice.prompt: Which state of matter keeps both a definite shape and a definite volume? (a) solid (b) liquid (c) gas (d) none of them
practice.answer: 1. (a) solid. Only solids keep both a fixed shape and a fixed volume because their particles are locked in place.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: In which state are the particles far apart and moving quickly in all directions? (a) solid (b) liquid (c) gas (d) ice
practice.answer: 2. (c) gas. Gas particles are spread far apart and move quickly in every direction.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A liquid poured from a cup into a bowl will: (a) change its amount (b) keep its shape (c) take the shape of the bowl (d) turn into a gas
practice.answer: 3. (c) take the shape of the bowl. A liquid keeps its volume but takes the container's shape; the amount does not change.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Melting is the change from: (a) liquid to solid (b) solid to liquid (c) liquid to gas (d) gas to liquid
practice.answer: 4. (b) solid to liquid. Melting is a solid becoming a liquid when heated.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which change needs heat to be REMOVED? (a) melting (b) evaporation (c) boiling (d) freezing
practice.answer: 5. (d) freezing. Freezing needs heat removed so particles slow and lock into a solid. The others all need heat added.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Evaporation happens: (a) only at boiling point (b) at any temperature, at the surface (c) only in a freezer (d) only at night
practice.answer: 6. (b) at any temperature, at the surface. Only surface particles with enough energy escape; boiling is not required.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which factor does NOT speed up evaporation? (a) more heat (b) more wind (c) a smaller surface area (d) drier air
practice.answer: 7. (c) a smaller surface area. A smaller surface slows evaporation; the other three all speed it up.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Water droplets forming on a cold glass is an example of: (a) melting (b) condensation (c) freezing (d) dissolving
practice.answer: 8. (b) condensation. Water vapour in the air cools on the glass and turns to liquid.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: In the water cycle, the sun's energy mainly causes: (a) precipitation (b) collection (c) evaporation (d) freezing
practice.answer: 9. (c) evaporation. The sun's heat drives water up from the sea and land as vapour.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: When sugar is stirred into tea and seems to vanish, it has: (a) melted (b) evaporated (c) dissolved (d) frozen
practice.answer: 10. (c) dissolved. The sugar particles spread among the water particles; it is still there (the tea tastes sweet).
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a gas? (a) a stone (b) camel milk (c) the air in a balloon (d) an ice cube
practice.answer: 11. (c) the air in a balloon. Air is a gas; the others are solids or a liquid.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Boiling is different from evaporation because boiling: (a) is very slow (b) happens only at the surface (c) is fast with bubbles all through the liquid (d) needs cooling
practice.answer: 12. (c) fast with bubbles all through the liquid. Boiling is rapid and happens throughout; evaporation is slow and only at the surface.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Sorting the States of Matter
activities.materials: A few solids: a stone, a wooden spoon, a metal key, a dried date.; A few liquids: water, cooking oil, camel milk or any milk.; A gas: a balloon or a plastic bag you can trap air in.; Two or three cups or bowls of different shapes.
steps: Take each solid and place it in different cups. Does it change shape? Does it keep its size? Record your answer.
Pour a little water into a tall cup, then into a wide bowl. Does the shape change? Does the amount change? Record it.
Do the same test with the cooking oil and the milk.
Blow air into the balloon or trap air in the bag. Squeeze it gently. Can you feel the gas pushing back? Does it fill the whole balloon?
Fill in your results table below.

activities.title: Watching Ice Melt (A State Change)
activities.materials: Two ice cubes of the same size (freeze water in a freezer if you have one).; Two small plates or saucers.; A clock or a phone timer.; A warm sunny spot and a cool shady spot.
steps: Place one ice cube on each plate.
Put one plate in a warm, sunny place and the other in a cool, shady place.
Start your timer. Every 5 minutes, look at both cubes and write down how much has melted.
Record the time when each cube has completely melted.

activities.title: The Great Evaporation Race
activities.materials: A wide shallow dish or plate.; A narrow tall cup or glass.; The same amount of water for each (measure with the same cup).; A marker or tape to mark the water level, and a sunny windowsill.
steps: Pour exactly the same amount of water into the wide dish and the tall cup.
Mark the starting water level on each container.
Place both in the same warm, sunny place, side by side, so temperature and wind are the same for both. The ONLY thing different is the surface area.
Check twice a day and mark the new water level with the time.
Continue until one container is empty. Record which dried up first.

activities.title: Catching Condensation
activities.materials: A clean glass or metal cup.; Cold water and, if you have them, a few ice cubes.; A dry cloth to wipe the outside of the cup first.
steps: Wipe the outside of the cup completely dry so you know it starts dry.
Fill the cup with cold water and add ice if you have it.
Place it on a table and wait 5 to 10 minutes without touching it.
Look closely at the outside of the cup. Gently touch it. What do you feel and see? Record it.

activities.title: A Mini Water Cycle in a Bowl
activities.materials: A large bowl.; A small empty cup that fits inside the bowl.; Warm (not boiling) water - ask an adult to help.; Cling film or a clear plastic bag to cover the bowl.; A small stone or pebble.
steps: Pour a little warm water into the bottom of the large bowl (this is your 'ocean'). Do not fill it.
Stand the empty small cup in the middle of the bowl, keeping it dry inside (this is your 'land').
Cover the whole bowl tightly with cling film so no air escapes.
Place the small stone on the cling film right above the empty cup, so the film dips down slightly toward the cup.
Put the bowl in a warm, sunny spot and watch for 30 to 60 minutes.

activities.title: Observe: Dissolving - When a Solid Seems to Disappear
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of dissolving - when a solid seems to disappear around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

terms: The tiny pieces, too small to see, that all matter is made of.
Solid to liquid, when heat is added (ice at 0 degrees Celsius).
Liquid to solid, when heat is removed (water at 0 degrees Celsius).
Liquid to gas at the surface, at any temperature.
Fast liquid to gas throughout the liquid, at the boiling point (water at 100 degrees Celsius).
Gas to liquid, when it cools.
Water in its invisible gas form.

vocabulary.example: At a certain temperature, called the melting point, they gain enough energy to break free from their fixed positions and begin sliding around each other.
The shallow pan spreads the water out over a much larger surface area than the deep bucket.
Because both temperature and air movement speed up evaporation, the clothes dry much faster than they would on a cool, still day.
Humidity is the amount of water vapour already in the air.

vocabulary.meaning: The tiny pieces, too small to see, that all matter is made of.
vocabulary.example: Explain the particle model - that all matter is made of tiny particles, and describe how those particles are arranged and how they move in each state.

vocabulary.meaning: Solid to liquid, when heat is added (ice at 0 degrees Celsius).
vocabulary.example: Describe state changes - melting, freezing, evaporation, condensing, and boiling - and explain how heating and cooling cause them.

vocabulary.meaning: Liquid to solid, when heat is removed (water at 0 degrees Celsius).
vocabulary.example: Describe state changes - melting, freezing, evaporation, condensing, and boiling - and explain how heating and cooling cause them.

vocabulary.meaning: Liquid to gas at the surface, at any temperature.
vocabulary.example: When Halima hangs the washing outside so it dries quickly in the hot afternoon sun, that is evaporation.

vocabulary.meaning: Fast liquid to gas throughout the liquid, at the boiling point (water at 100 degrees Celsius).
vocabulary.example: Describe state changes - melting, freezing, evaporation, condensing, and boiling - and explain how heating and cooling cause them.

vocabulary.meaning: Gas to liquid, when it cools.
vocabulary.example: Describe the water cycle and explain how evaporation and condensation move water between the ocean, the sky, and the land.

vocabulary.meaning: Water in its invisible gas form.
vocabulary.example: Water is a solid when it is frozen as ice, a liquid when you drink it, and a gas when it becomes water vapour.

commonMistakes: It says evaporation only happens at 100 degrees.
A dried puddle's water is destroyed.
It ignores that matter is not destroyed.
The white cloud above a pot is water vapour.
Dissolved sugar has melted or gone.
Melting and dissolving are mixed up.
You cannot see them, so they seem like nothing.

connections.text: Living things (earlier units): plants need liquid water to grow, and they release water vapour from their leaves (transpiration), adding to the water cycle.
the water cycle creates clouds, rain, and the Gu and Deyr rainy seasons.
measuring temperature, volume, and time, and drawing graphs of evaporation results.
cooking, drying washing and fish, boiling water to make it safe, and making salt from seawater.
understanding the water cycle helps us value and protect clean water as good caretakers (khalifah) of the earth.

fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 6. (b) at any temperature, at the surface. Only surface particles with enough energy escape; boiling is not required.

fluency.prompt: Which factor does NOT speed up evaporation? (a) more heat (b) more wind (c) a smaller surface area (d) drier air
fluency.answer: 7. (c) a smaller surface area. A smaller surface slows evaporation; the other three all speed it up.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 7. (c) a smaller surface area. A smaller surface slows evaporation; the other three all speed it up.

fluency.prompt: Water droplets forming on a cold glass is an example of: (a) melting (b) condensation (c) freezing (d) dissolving
fluency.answer: 8. (b) condensation. Water vapour in the air cools on the glass and turns to liquid.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 8. (b) condensation. Water vapour in the air cools on the glass and turns to liquid.

fluency.prompt: In the water cycle, the sun's energy mainly causes: (a) precipitation (b) collection (c) evaporation (d) freezing
fluency.answer: 9. (c) evaporation. The sun's heat drives water up from the sea and land as vapour.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 9. (c) evaporation. The sun's heat drives water up from the sea and land as vapour.

fluency.prompt: When sugar is stirred into tea and seems to vanish, it has: (a) melted (b) evaporated (c) dissolved (d) frozen
fluency.answer: 10. (c) dissolved. The sugar particles spread among the water particles; it is still there (the tea tastes sweet).
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 10. (c) dissolved. The sugar particles spread among the water particles; it is still there (the tea tastes sweet).

fluency.prompt: Which of these is a gas? (a) a stone (b) camel milk (c) the air in a balloon (d) an ice cube
fluency.answer: 11. (c) the air in a balloon. Air is a gas; the others are solids or a liquid.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 11. (c) the air in a balloon. Air is a gas; the others are solids or a liquid.

fluency.prompt: Boiling is different from evaporation because boiling: (a) is very slow (b) happens only at the surface (c) is fast with bubbles all through the liquid (d) needs cooling
fluency.answer: 12. (c) fast with bubbles all through the liquid. Boiling is rapid and happens throughout; evaporation is slow and only at the surface.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 12. (c) fast with bubbles all through the liquid. Boiling is rapid and happens throughout; evaporation is slow and only at the surface.

realProblems.prompt: Halima wants the washing to dry as fast as possible. Give her three pieces of advice based on the factors affecting evaporation, and explain each one.
realProblems.answer: 1. (i) Hang the washing in the hot sun - heat gives particles energy to escape. (ii) Spread the clothes out wide - a larger surface area lets more water escape. (iii) Hang them where the wind blows - moving air carries damp air away so more water can evaporate. (Drier air / low humidity also helps.)
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1. (i) Hang the washing in the hot sun - heat gives particles energy to escape. (ii) Spread the clothes out wide - a larger surface area lets more water escape. (iii) Hang them where the wind blows - moving air carries damp air away so more water can evaporate. (Drier air / low humidity also helps.)

realProblems.prompt: Yusuf boils water on the charcoal jiko before the family drinks it. What state change happens as the water boils, and why does boiling help make the water safe?
realProblems.answer: 2. The water changes from liquid to gas (boiling/evaporation), and steam rises. Boiling heats the water to 100 degrees Celsius, which kills harmful germs in it, making the water safe to drink once it cools.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 2. The water changes from liquid to gas (boiling/evaporation), and steam rises. Boiling heats the water to 100 degrees Celsius, which kills harmful germs in it, making the water safe to drink once it cools.

realProblems.prompt: During the dry season a small river near Garissa shrinks and almost disappears, then fills again after the Gu rains. Explain both events using the water cycle.
realProblems.answer: 3. In the dry season, hot sun causes fast evaporation and little rain falls, so the river shrinks. When the Gu rains come, the water cycle delivers precipitation (rain), which collects and refills the river.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 3. In the dry season, hot sun causes fast evaporation and little rain falls, so the river shrinks. When the Gu rains come, the water cycle delivers precipitation (rain), which collects and refills the river.

realProblems.prompt: Amina spills a cup of water on a hot day and a cup of water inside a cool shaded room. Which puddle will disappear first, and why?
realProblems.answer: 4. The spilled water on the hot day disappears first, because higher temperature gives the water particles more energy to evaporate quickly. The cool shaded puddle evaporates much more slowly.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 4. The spilled water on the hot day disappears first, because higher temperature gives the water particles more energy to evaporate quickly. The cool shaded puddle evaporates much more slowly.

realProblems.prompt: A family wants to make salt from seawater using the sun. Describe the steps and name the two science ideas (from this unit) that make it work.
realProblems.answer: 5. Pour seawater into wide, shallow pans and leave them in the strong sun. The water evaporates (a large surface area and heat make this fast), leaving the salt behind. The two ideas are dissolving (salt was dissolved in the seawater) and evaporation (which removes the water and leaves the salt).
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 5. Pour seawater into wide, shallow pans and leave them in the strong sun. The water evaporates (a large surface area and heat make this fast), leaving the salt behind. The two ideas are dissolving (salt was dissolved in the seawater) and evaporation (which removes the water and leaves the salt).

realProblems.prompt: Two identical cups of water are left out. One is covered with a lid and one is open. After two days the open cup has much less water. Explain why, using particles.
realProblems.answer: 1. In the open cup, water particles that escape from the surface drift away into the room, so evaporation continues and the water level drops. In the covered cup, escaped particles are trapped, the air above fills with vapour, and many fall back in, so little water is lost.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1. In the open cup, water particles that escape from the surface drift away into the room, so evaporation continues and the water level drops. In the covered cup, escaped particles are trapped, the air above fills with vapour, and many fall back in, so little water is lost.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Describe how the particles are arranged in a solid.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1. In a solid the particles are packed closely together in a fixed, regular pattern and only vibrate on the spot. This is why solids keep their shape.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain why a liquid can be poured but a solid cannot.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 2. In a liquid the particles can slide past one another, so a liquid flows and can be poured. In a solid the particles are locked in place, so it cannot flow.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What happens to the particles of ice when it is heated until it melts?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 3. When ice is heated, its particles vibrate faster until they gain enough energy to break free and slide around each other. The solid ice becomes liquid water.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the four factors that affect how fast a liquid evaporates.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 4. Temperature, surface area, air movement (wind), and humidity.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why do wet clothes dry faster on a hot, windy day than on a cool, still day?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 5. Heat gives the water particles more energy to escape, and wind carries the damp air away so more particles can leave. Both factors speed up evaporation.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between evaporation and boiling?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 6. Evaporation is slow, happens only at the surface, and can occur at any temperature. Boiling is fast, happens throughout the liquid with bubbles, and occurs at the boiling point (100 degrees Celsius for water).

questions.question: Which state of matter keeps both a definite shape and a definite volume?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: solid. Only solids keep both a fixed shape and a fixed volume because their particles are locked in place.

questions.question: In which state are the particles far apart and moving quickly in all directions?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: gas. Gas particles are spread far apart and move quickly in every direction.

questions.question: A liquid poured from a cup into a bowl will:
options: take the shape of the bowl
questions.answer: take the shape of the bowl
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: take the shape of the bowl. A liquid keeps its volume but takes the container's shape; the amount does not change.

questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.

selfAssessment: I can name and describe the three states of matter: solid, liquid, and gas, and give examples of each from your own home.
I can explain the particle model - that all matter is made of tiny particles, and describe how those particles are arranged and how they move in each state.
I can describe state changes - melting, freezing, evaporation, condensing, and boiling - and explain how heating and cooling cause them.
I can explain evaporation and identify the four main factors that make a liquid evaporate faster: temperature, surface area, air movement, and humidity.
I can describe the water cycle and explain how evaporation and condensation move water between the ocean, the sky, and the land.
I can plan a fair test to investigate what affects evaporation, and record your results carefully.
I can use scientific words correctly when you write and talk about matter.
I can name two other factors mentioned in the passage that help the fish dry faster.
